content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
, FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Larry Bush 
245 Diamond Street 
San Francisco, CA 94114 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-161 
Dear Mr. Bush: 
July 3, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a member of the 
Citizens' General Obligation Bond Oversight Committee (the "Oversight Committee") under 
Section 84308 of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Note that the Commission does not address hypothetical scenarios or third party requests for 
advice. Under the Act, a requestor may only seek advice about his or her own duties unless he or 
she is the authorized representative of another. Since you have not provided any facts regarding 
your authorization to request advice on behalf of a member of a city council or the Board of 
Supervisors, we limit our advice to that pertaining to your position on the Oversight Committee. 
Moreover, we are only providing advice under the Act and not advice concerning any local 
laws that may apply. In addition, note the Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. If the facts underlying these decisions should be inaccurate the advice would not be 
immunizing. 
FACTS 
You are a local San Francisco official (a member of the Oversight Committee) and you file 
a Form 700. Per the Oversight Committee's website: 
"On March 5th, 2002 the San Francisco voters adopted Proposition F, the Citizen 
Oversight ofBonq Expenditures Initiative. The Ordinance established a 
committee of nine members for the purpose of infonning the public concerning 
the expenditure of general bond proceeds through active review and the 
publishing of regular reports. The ordinance required that the nine members meet 
certain minimum qualifications and be appointed as follows: three members by 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-161 
Page No. 2 
the Mayor, three members by the Board of Supervisors, two members by the 
Controller and one member by the Civil Grand Jury. Each member serves for a 
term of two years and may be re-appointed for a second two-year term. 
"On November 4, 2003, the voters added to the duties of the Committee by 
approving Proposition C, which requires the Controller to serve as the City 
Services Auditor. The Ordinance also added to the duties of the Committee by 
requiring that it serve as a Citizens Audit Review Board. In this role, the 
Committee provides advisory input to the Controller on matters set forth in the 
Ordinance, including requiring that the Committee: I) review the Controller's 
service standards and benchmarks to ensure their accuracy and usefulness; 2) 
review all audits to assure they meet requirements set forth in the Ordinance; 3) 
review complaints received through the Controller's Whistleblower Hotline and 
their disposition; and 4) when appropriate, hold public hearings regarding the 
results of benchmark studies and audits." 
You stated that: 
• At various times, you have served as the Treasurer of a campaign committee and that you 
have also solicited contributions on behalf of the campaign committee. In an earlier email 
communication, you identified the committee as Friends of Ethics, Yes on T. 
• You stated that you have also solicited and accepted payments to benefit an entity not 
involved in a political campaign. You did not specify the entity for which you solicited 
payments. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does your status as a treasurer or principal officer of a campaign committee create a 
presumption that you have sought or accepted contributions that are subject to Section 84308? 
2. Does Section 84308 apply to you on the Oversight Committee if you you solicit or accept 
contributions for your election to the Democratic County Central Committee? 
3. Does Section 84308 apply to soliciting or accepting contributions from lobbyists for 
clients who may have pending matters before the Oversight Committee? 
4. How is "agent" defined for the purposes of application Qf Section 84308. 
5. Does Section 84308 apply to "behest" payments made at the behest of an official but 
contributed to a nonprofit or governmental purpose? 
CONCLUSIONS AND ANALYSIS 
Section 84308 was added to the Act to ensure that appointed members of certain agencies 
(boards or commissions) would not be biased by campaign contributors or potential contributors 


File No. A-17-161 
Page No. 3 
who might appear before them in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for 
use. Section 84308(a)(3) clarifies the agency subject to the rule: 
'" Agency' means an agency as defined in Section 82003 except that it does not 
include the courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government, local 
governmental agencies whose members are directly elected by the voters, the 
Legislature, the Board of Equalization, or constitutional officers. However, this 
section applies to any person who is a member of an exempted agency but is 
acting as a voting member of another agency." 
Section 84308 provides two sets of requirements on officers of the agencies subject to the 
section: 
(I) The Pendency Prohibition: No officer shall accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of 
more than $250, from any party,2 or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, 
while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the 
agency and for three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding if the 
officer knows or has reason to know that the participant has a financial interest. 
{2) Disclosure and Disqualificatio11: If an officer of the agency has received a contribution 
within the preceding 12 months of more than $250 from a party or from any participant the officer 
shall: 
• 
Disclosure: Prior to any decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other 
entitlement for use pending before an agency, each officer of the agency who received a 
contribution within the preceding 12 months in an amount of more than $250 from a party 
or from any participant shall disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding. 
• 
Disqualification: An officer of an agency shall not make, participate in making, or in any 
way attempt to use his or her official position to influence the decision in the proceeding 
involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use. 
Section 84308 may apply to some decisions of the Oversight Committee. Section 84308 
expressly applies to any decision in the proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement 
for use, including all business, professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other 
entitlements for use, including all entitlements for land use, all contracts (other than competitively 
bid, labor, or personal employment contracts), and all franchises. {Section 84308(a){5).) 
2 A "party" is "any person who files an application for, or is the subject of, a proceeding involving a license, 
pennit, or other entitlement for use." (Section 84308(a){l).) A "participant" is: "any person who is not a party but who 
actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding involving a license, pennit, or other entitlement for 
use and who has a financial interest in the decision as described in Article l {commencing with Section 87100) of 
Chapter 7. A person actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding if he or she lobbies in person the 
officers or employees of the agency, testifies in person before the agency, or otherwise acts to influence officers of the 
agency." 


File No. A-17-161 
Page No. 4 
Moreover, Regulation 18438.2 states that a "proceeding involving a license, pennit or other 
entitlement for use" includes any proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modify a license, 
pennit or other entitlement for use. 
1. Does your status as a treasurer or principal officer of a campaign committee create a 
presumption that you have sought or accepted contributions that are subject to Section 84308? 
Your question refers to the rules concerning an officer's conduct during the pendency of the 
proceeding. Note that the disclosure/disqualification rule only applies to the receipt of contributions 
and not the solicitation of contributions to others. With respect to solicitation under the pendency 
rule, Regulation l8438.6(c) states: 
"An officer 'solicits' a contribution only if he or she knows or has reason 
to know that the person being solicited for a contribution is a party or agent of a 
party, or is a participant or agent of a participant and either: 
"( 1) The officer or candidate personally requests a contribution for his or 
her own campaign or controlled committee, or for any other candidate, public 
official or committee, either orally or in writing; or 
"(2) The agent of the officer or candidate with the officer or candidate's 
knowledge, requests a contribution for the officer or candidate's own campaign or 
controlled committee. 
"(d) Notwithstanding subsection (c), a solicitation does not include a 
request made in a mass mailing sent to members of the public, to a public 
gathering, or published in a newspaper, on radio or TV, or in any other mass 
media." 
This is a factual test, there is no presumption in the statute or interpretive regulations. 
2. Does Section 84308 apply to you on the Oversight Committee if you you solicit or accept 
contributions for your election to the Democratic County Central Committee? 
Per Regulation l 8438.6(a) a person "accepts" or "receives" a contribution only if the 
contribution is for that person's own candidacy or own controlled committee. Section 82007 defines 
"candidate'' to mean an individual who is listed on the ballot or who has qualified to have write-in 
votes on his or her behalf counted by electiol) officials, for nomination for or election to any 
elective office, or who receives a contribution or makes an expenditure or gives his or her consent 
for any other person to receive a contribution or make an expenditure with a view to bringing about 
his or her nomination or election to any elective office, whether or not the specific elective office 
for which he or she will seek nomination or election is known at the time the contribution is 
received or the expenditure is made and whether or not he or she has announced his or her 
candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy. 


File No. A-17-161 
Page No. 5 
Section 82023 defines "elective office" as any state, regional, county, municipal, district or 
judicial office that is filled at an election. "'Elective office' also includes membership on a county 
central committee of a qualified political party .... " 
Therefore, if you receive contributions for your election to Democratic County Central 
Committee, these contributions could trigger Section 84308. 
3. Does Section 84308 apply to soliciting or accepting contributions ji-om lobbyists for 
clients who may have pending matters before the Oversight Committee? 
Section 84308 applies to any contributor that meets the criteria in the statute. Note that the 
Act's lobbyist rules do not apply to local officials, so you should consult with your agency counsel 
to determine if any local rules apply to contributions from lobbyists. 
4. How is "agent" defined for the pwposes of application of Section 84308. 
Regulation 18438.3 defines "agent" for purposes of Section 84308 as follows: 
"(a) For purposes of Government Code Section 84308, a person is the 'agent' of a 
party to, or a participant in, a proceeding involving a license, permit or other 
entitlement for use only ifhe or she represents that person in connection with the 
proceeding involving the license, permit or other entitlement for use. If an 
individual acting as an agent is also acting as an employee or member of a law, 
architectural, engineering or consulting firm, or a similar entity or corporation, 
both the entity or corporation and the individual are "agents." 
''(b) To determine whether a contribution of more than $250 has been made by a 
person or his or her agent, contributions made by that person within the preceding 
12 months shall be aggregated with those made by his or her agent within the 
preceding 12 months or the period of the agency relationship, whichever is 
shorter." 
5. Does Section 84308 apply to "behest" payments made at the behest of an official but 
contributed to a nonprofit or governme11tal pwpose? 
As noted above, the disclosure/disqualification rule only applies to contributions you 
receive. With respect to solicitation under the pendency rule, Regulation l8438.6(c) states: "The 
pendency prohibition applies to sqlicitations for contributions for the officer's own campajgn or 
controlled committee, or for any other·candidate, public official or committee." Moreover, 
"contribution" is specifically defined in Section 82015 as a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a 
payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise made for a political purpose. 
Consequently, payments solicited for purposes other than political purposes would not trigger the 
statute. 


:jgl 
File No. A-17-161 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
} 
,/' 
_,,.--
/'' 
(
/ y: 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel, 
Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
S AND ANALYSIS 
Section 84308 was added to the Act to ensure that appointed members of certain agencies 
(boards or commissions) would not be biased by campaign contributors or potential contributors

section applies to any person who is a member of an exempted agency but is 
acting as a voting member of another agency." 
Section 84308 provides two sets of requirements on officers of the agencies subject to the 
section: 
(I) The Pendency Prohibition: No officer shall accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of 
more than $250, from any party,2 or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, 
while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the 
agency and for three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding if the

{2) Disclosure and Disqualificatio11: If an officer of the agency has received a contribution 
within the preceding 12 months of more than $250 from a party or from any participant the officer 
shall: 
•

expressly applies to any decision in the proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement 
for use, including all business, professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other 
entitlements for use, including all entitlements for land use, all contracts (other than competitively 
bid, labor, or personal employment contracts), and all franchises. {Section 84308(a){5).) 
2 A "party" is "any person who files an application for, or is the subject of, a proceeding involving a license, 
pennit, or other entitlement for use." (Section 84308(a){l).) A "participant" is: "any person who is not a party but who

use and who has a financial interest in the decision as described in Article l {commencing with Section 87100) of 
Chapter 7. A person actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding if he or she lobbies in person the 
officers or employees of the agency, testifies in person before the agency, or otherwise acts to influence officers of the 
agency."  
Moreover, Regulation 18438.2 states that a "proceeding involving a license, pennit or other 
entitlement for use" includes any proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modify a license, 
pennit or other entitlement for use.

sections.facts:
You are a local San Francisco official (a member of the Oversight Committee) and you file 
a Form 700. Per the Oversight Committee's website: 
"On March 5th, 2002 the San Francisco voters adopted Proposition F, the Citizen 
Oversight ofBonq Expenditures Initiative. The Ordinance established a 
committee of nine members for the purpose of infonning the public concerning 
the expenditure of general bond proceeds through active review and the 
publishing of regular reports. The ordinance required that the nine members meet 
certain minimum qualifications and be appointed as follows: three members by 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained inAll 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.  
the Mayor, three members by the Board of Supervisors, two members by the 
Controller and one member by the Civil Grand Jury. Each member serves for a 
term of two years and may be re-appointed for a second two-year term. 
"On November 4, 2003, the voters added to the duties of the Committee by 
approving Proposition C, which requires the Controller to serve as the City 
Services Auditor. The Ordinance also added to the duties of the Committee by 
requiring that it serve as a Citizens Audit Review Board. In this role, the 
Committee provides advisory input to the Controller on matters set forth in the 
Ordinance, including requiring that the Committee: I) review the Controller's 
service standards and benchmarks to ensure their accuracy and usefulness; 2) 
review all audits to assure they meet requirements set forth in the Ordinance; 3) 
review complaints received through the Controller's Whistleblower Hotline and 
their disposition; and 4) when appropriate, hold public hearings regarding the 
results of benchmark studies and audits." 
You stated that: 
• At various times, you have served as the Treasurer of a campaign committee and that you 
have also solicited contributions on behalf of the campaign committee. In an earlier email 
communication, you identified the committee as Friends of Ethics, Yes on T. 
• You stated that you have also solicited and accepted payments to benefit an entity not 
involved in a political campaign. You did not specify the entity for which you solicited 
payments.

5. Does Section 84308 apply to "behest" payments made at the behest of an official but 
contributed to a nonprofit or governme11tal pwpose? 
As noted above, the disclosure/disqualification rule only applies to contributions you 
receive. With respect to solicitation under the pendency rule, Regulation l8438.6(c) states: "The 
pendency prohibition applies to sqlicitations for contributions for the officer's own campajgn or 
controlled committee, or for any other·candidate, public official or committee." Moreover, 
"contribution" is specifically defined in Section 82015 as a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a 
payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise made for a political purpose. 
Consequently, payments solicited for purposes other than political purposes would not trigger the 
statute. 


:jgl
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA , FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Larry Bush 245 Diamond Street San Francisco, CA 94114 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-161 Dear Mr. Bush: July 3, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a member of the Citizens' General Obligation Bond Oversight Committee (the "Oversight Committee") under Section 84308 of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Note that the Commission does not address hypothetical scenarios or third party requests for advice. Under the Act, a requestor may only seek advice about his or her own duties unless he or she is the authorized representative of another. Since you have not provided any facts regarding your authorization to request advice on behalf of a member of a city council or the Board of Supervisors, we limit our advice to that pertaining to your position on the Oversight Committee. Moreover, we are only providing advice under the Act and not advice concerning any local laws that may apply. In addition, note the Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If the facts underlying these decisions should be inaccurate the advice would not be immunizing. FACTS You are a local San Francisco official (a member of the Oversight Committee) and you file a Form 700. Per the Oversight Committee's website: "On March 5th, 2002 the San Francisco voters adopted Proposition F, the Citizen Oversight ofBonq Expenditures Initiative. The Ordinance established a committee of nine members for the purpose of infonning the public concerning the expenditure of general bond proceeds through active review and the publishing of regular reports. The ordinance required that the nine members meet certain minimum qualifications and be appointed as follows: three members by 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-161 Page No. 2 the Mayor, three members by the Board of Supervisors, two members by the Controller and one member by the Civil Grand Jury. Each member serves for a term of two years and may be re-appointed for a second two-year term. "On November 4, 2003, the voters added to the duties of the Committee by approving Proposition C, which requires the Controller to serve as the City Services Auditor. The Ordinance also added to the duties of the Committee by requiring that it serve as a Citizens Audit Review Board. In this role, the Committee provides advisory input to the Controller on matters
